title: Phonon-driven nodal surface superconductivity of Fermi arcs
authors: Francesco Buccheri, Alessandro de Martino, Jeroen van den Brink
abstract: According to recent observations, the topological surface states of Weyl semimetals may develop a superconducting gap, while bulk superconductivity remains absent. What drives the formation of this novel superconducting state is an open question. Here, we show that this phenomenon can arise from the interaction of Fermi arc electrons with both surface and bulk phonons in time-reversal-invariant Weyl semimetals. We identify two competing pairing channels, intra-arc and inter-arc, whose relative strength is governed by the efficiency of Coulomb screening at the surface. The combined effect of the Fermi arcs being disconnected and the weak screening of the Coulomb repulsion at the system's surface causes nodes to appear in the superconducting gap, as observed recently by photoelectron spectroscopy experiments on PtBi2. This suggests manipulation of the Coulomb screening, e.g. by a surface layer coating, as a pathway to engineer the critical temperature, as well as size and symmetry of the surface superconducting gap.
summary: This work theoretically explains how phonon interactions, combined with Coulomb repulsion, can induce superconductivity exclusively on the topological Fermi arcs of Weyl semimetals. The key finding is that weak surface screening favors an inter-arc pairing channel, which results in the observationally important nodal superconducting gap structure. This suggests that surface engineering of the dielectric environment can control the superconducting properties.
main_problem: The paper addresses the mechanism driving the formation of a superconducting gap specifically on the topological surface states (Fermi arcs) of Weyl semimetals, while the bulk remains non-superconducting.
main_result: Nodal superconductivity arises generically from the combination of phonon-mediated attraction and weakly screened long-range Coulomb repulsion acting on disconnected Fermi-arc segments.
method: The authors use a continuum description, deriving effective electron-electron interactions (Coulomb and electron-phonon) and solving the resulting gap equation in the mean-field approximation.
model_or_system: The system is time-reversal-invariant Weyl semimetals, modeled using a tight-binding approach that yields surface Fermi arcs. The theory incorporates coupling to both surface and bulk phonons and long-range Coulomb interactions.
key_observables: Superconducting gap structure (nodal vs. nodeless), critical temperature ($T_c$), and the relative strengths of intra-arc and inter-arc pairing channels.
important_parameters: Coulomb screening strength ($q_s$), phonon frequencies ($\omega$), and the geometry of the Fermi arcs.
main_assumptions: The bulk density of states is negligible compared to the surface DOS near the chemical potential, and the analysis relies on linearizing the gap equation near $T_c$.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the Fermi arcs in the surface Brillouin zone, the competition between intra-arc and inter-arc scattering processes, and the dependence of $T_c$ on the screening momentum.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first deriving the electronic structure and the electron-phonon coupling Hamiltonian, then formulating the effective electron-electron interaction, and finally solving the gap equation by distinguishing between intra-arc and inter-arc pairing channels to determine the superconducting gap symmetry and $T_c$.
why_interesting: The detailed theoretical framework for deriving pairing mechanisms from lattice vibrations and surface topology is highly relevant for understanding emergent superconducting states in topological materials, bridging concepts from lattice dynamics and superconductivity theory.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

The shaded region indicates the surface BZ in which the exponential solution corresponding to a pair of Fermi-arc states is normalizable for z < 0.

FIG. 2. Left: illustration of the intra- (blue) and inter-arc (green) scattering processes in the Cooper channel, with ini- tial and final states on the Fermi arcs. Right: the nodal and node-less solutions (normalized to their maximum value) of the gap equation (7) projected to the Fermi surface, in pres- ence of intra- (blue) or inter- (green) arc scattering. Here α = 0.05, Vep = 0.025Dk2 W .

FIG. 3. Critical temperature for intra- (solid) and inter- (dashed) arc pairing, as a function of the Thomas-Fermi screening momentum. For weak screening inter-arc pairing prevails and the gap is nodal. Here α = 0.05 and three values of the bare electron-phonon interaction rae Vep/Dk2 W = 0.025 (blue), 0.035 (green), 0.045 (orange), 0.055 (purple).
